content.text: This is a solid, coherent set of changes. Before writing code I need to see how the existing pickers, job briefs, and the auxiliary registry are wired so the new macro picker matches the established pattern exactly. Let me read the key files.

content.content:
1	/**
2	 * Auxiliary model registry.
3	 *
4	 * Auxiliary models are admin-selectable model choices that are not one of the
5	 * three strict pipeline roles. They are explicit single-pick slots in this
6	 * pass: unset or unresolvable settings fail loudly instead of using a shipped
7	 * hidden default.
8	 */
9	
10	import {
11	  GENERAL_PURPOSE_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
12	  VISION_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
13	  VISION_REASONING_EFFORT_SETTING_KEY,
14	  TRANSLATION_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
15	} from './defaults';
16	
17	/**
18	 * A single model capability the picker can filter on. These names match the
19	 * capability columns on model_capabilities and the ModelInfo shape returned by
20	 * /api/admin/models.
21	 */
22	export type AuxiliaryCapabilityFilter =
23	  | 'vision'
24	  | 'thinking'
25	  | 'tools'
26	  | 'structuredOutputs'
27	  | 'promptCaching'
28	  | 'outputCap'
29	  | 'pdf';
30	
31	export interface AuxiliaryModelDef {
32	  /** Stable id used as the GUI `?stage=` param and the resolver lookup key. */
33	  id: string;
34	  /** Settings row holding the chosen route/model id. */
35	  routeSettingKey: string;
36	  /** Optional settings row for reasoning effort. Omit when effort is irrelevant. */
37	  reasoningEffortSettingKey?: string;
38	  /** Capability the picker filters the model list on. Omit for any model. */
39	  requiredCapability?: AuxiliaryCapabilityFilter;
40	  /** Short plain-text label. The GUI may render richer copy keyed by id. */
41	  label: string;
42	}
43	
44	export const VISION_AUXILIARY_MODEL: AuxiliaryModelDef = {
45	  id: 'vision',
46	  routeSettingKey: VISION_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
47	  reasoningEffortSettingKey: VISION_REASONING_EFFORT_SETTING_KEY,
48	  requiredCapability: 'vision',
49	  label: 'Image vision model',
50	};
51	
52	export const GENERAL_PURPOSE_AUXILIARY_MODEL: AuxiliaryModelDef = {
53	  id: 'general',
54	  routeSettingKey: GENERAL_PURPOSE_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
55	  label: 'General-purpose model',
56	};
57	
58	export const TRANSLATION_AUXILIARY_MODEL: AuxiliaryModelDef = {
59	  id: 'translation',
60	  routeSettingKey: TRANSLATION_ROUTE_SETTING_KEY,
61	  label: 'Translation model',
62	};
63	
64	/** Order here is the order the auxiliary cards render in the admin settings page. */
65	export const AUXILIARY_MODELS: AuxiliaryModelDef[] = [
66	  VISION_AUXILIARY_MODEL,
67	  GENERAL_PURPOSE_AUXILIARY_MODEL,
68	  TRANSLATION_AUXILIARY_MODEL,
69	];
70	
71	export function getAuxiliaryModelDef(id: string): AuxiliaryModelDef | undefined {
72	  return AUXILIARY_MODELS.find((a) => a.id === id);
73	}
74	
75	/** Set of auxiliary ids, convenient for membership tests. */
76	export const AUXILIARY_MODEL_IDS: ReadonlySet<string> = new Set(
77	  AUXILIARY_MODELS.map((a) => a.id),
78	);
79

The picker is filtered to{' '} 102 vision-capable models from any provider. The shipped 103 default is the Gemini extraction model; change it here with no redeploy. 104 105 ), 106 settingDescription: 'Vision model used to transcribe uploaded images to text before extraction.', 107 effortSettingDescription: 108 'Reasoning effort for image transcription. Most vision models ignore this; leave Off unless the chosen model benefits.', 109 }, 110 general: { 111 subtitle: 'Utility / fallback model for internal jobs', 112 description: ( 113 <> 114 Used for one-off internal tasks that don't fit the three stages 115 above — taxonomy cluster naming, on-demand classification, ad-hoc admin 116 queries. Pick whatever the current best general-purpose model is and 117 update it here when a newer one ships. The picker draws from the full 118 discovered catalog (no pool or stage requirements apply). 119 120 ), 121 settingDescription: 'General-purpose / utility model. Admin updates this when a newer model is available.', 122 }, 123 translation: { 124 subtitle: 'Translates approved claims into employees’ languages', 125 description: ( 126 <> 127 When a claim is approved, this model translates its summary into the 128 other supported language(s) so China-group employees read claims in 129 Simplified Chinese while everyone else reads English (one unified 130 knowledge graph). Evidence quotes and the synthesized Brain stay in their 131 original language. The picker draws from the full discovered catalog — pick 132 a strong Chinese-native model (e.g. Qwen) for best 133 Mandarin quality. The shipped default is the multilingual Sonnet route. 134 135 ), 136 settingDescription: 'Model used to translate approved claim summaries for the bilingual claim layer.', 137 }, 138 }; 139 140 const VISION_MODEL_BRIEF = `THE ORACLE — "Image Vision Model" role brief 141 (Paste this into a model-evaluation assistant or vendor comparison to judge whether a given model is the right pick for this setting.) 142 143 PURPOSE 144 This model performs Pass 1 of The Oracle's image-ingestion pipeline: it converts a single uploaded image into a faithful, self-contained PLAIN-TEXT rendering. It does NOT create knowledge itself — its text output is handed to a separate extraction model that mines structured "claims" from it. Its only job is high-fidelity image-to-text transcription and description. Everything downstream depends on this text being complete and accurate. 145 146 WHERE IT SITS IN THE PIPELINE 147 An employee uploads an image in a channel -> this model transcribes it to text -> the text is chunked, embedded, and stored as document_chunks -> a separate extraction model pulls operational claims, and each claim must quote this text exactly (deterministic quote-validation). If this model omits or hallucinates content, the knowledge base becomes silently wrong. Fidelity matters more than eloquence. 148 149 WHAT IT IS FED (input) 150 - Exactly ONE raw image per call, sent inline (PNG, JPEG, WebP, or HEIC). 151 - A fixed system instruction telling it to transcribe verbatim and describe visual meaning. 152 - A one-line request naming the file. 153 - No conversation history, no other documents, no tools. 154 Typical images: process diagrams, flowcharts, org charts, spreadsheets/tables, screenshots of apps or emails, photos of printed documents, whiteboards, shipping labels, handwritten notes, product photos. 155 156 WHAT IS EXPECTED (output) 157 - Plain text only — no markdown code fences, no "Here is..." preamble. 158 - VERBATIM transcription of every piece of visible text: labels, headings, every table cell, captions, callouts, stamps, axis labels, legends, handwriting. 159 - Diagrams/flowcharts/org charts rendered as an explicit TEXT TOPOLOGY — nodes as [Shape/Color: "verbatim label"], edges as annotated directional arrows ([A] --(condition)--> [B]), swimlanes/columns as headers — NOT free-form prose. Tables rendered row-by-row. Verbatim labels kept inside the nodes so they stay exactly quotable. 160 - Concrete operational content surfaced: rules, steps, routing, ownership, dates, quantities, statuses, conditions, exceptions. 161 - Honesty about uncertainty: explicitly flag anything unreadable or ambiguous; never invent detail. 162 163 PROCESS IT USES 164 A single, stateless, one-shot vision call. No caching (each image is unique), no tool calls, no multi-turn, no structured/JSON output. Just: see the image, emit faithful text. 165 166 WHAT MAKES A MODEL PERFECTLY SUITED 167 - Excellent OCR-grade text extraction, including dense tables and messy or handwritten text. 168 - Strong document and diagram understanding — reads charts, flowcharts, forms, and layouts, not just loose text. 169 - High instruction-following discipline: verbatim fidelity, no embellishment, no hallucination, and willingness to say "unreadable." 170 - Preserves reading order and table structure when rendering to prose. 171 - Generous max OUTPUT length, enough to fully render dense, text-heavy images. 172 - Low cost and reasonable latency: this runs once per uploaded image at volume and is asynchronous (no human waiting), so throughput and cost matter more than sub-second speed. 173 - High reliability and availability. 174 - Multilingual text recognition if the business handles non-English documents. 175 176 CAPABILITIES IT NEEDS (hard requirements) 177 - Image input (vision) — non-negotiable. 178 - Plain-text generation with strong instruction adherence. 179 - Sufficient max output tokens to transcribe a full page of dense content. 180 181 CAPABILITIES IT DOES NOT NEED (do not pay for these; some actively hurt) 182 - Tool / function calling — unused. 183 - Structured outputs / JSON schema / response_format — output is prose, not schema. 184 - Extended "thinking" / reasoning budget — transcription is perception, not deliberation; reasoning mostly adds latency and cost with no fidelity gain. Keep reasoning effort Off or Low. 185 - Large context window — input is one image plus a short prompt, nowhere near long-context territory. 186 - Streaming — output is consumed by a background worker, not rendered live. 187 - PDF input — PDFs are handled by a separate parsing path, not this model. 188 - Audio / video — out of scope. 189 190 BOTTOM LINE 191 Pick the cheapest, most reliable vision model with the best OCR plus document/diagram comprehension and the strictest instruction-following. Ignore reasoning, tools, structured output, and long context — they are irrelevant here, and reasoning effort should stay low.`; 192 193 const INTERVIEW_MODEL_BRIEF = `THE ORACLE — "Interview Model" role brief 194 (Paste this into a model-evaluation assistant or vendor comparison to judge whether a given model is the right pick for this setting.) 195 196 PURPOSE 197 This model is The Oracle's employee-facing conversation engine. It answers direct chat messages, asks clarifying questions, and helps employees surface operational knowledge without pretending the knowledge graph contains facts it does not contain. It is the human interface to the system, so tone, latency, groundedness, and restraint all matter. 198 199 WHERE IT SITS IN THE PIPELINE 200 An employee sends a chat message -> the app deterministically retrieves recent conversation, open gaps, approved claims, Brain snippets, and relevant evidence -> this model writes the assistant reply -> the reply and usage metadata are stored for audit. It does NOT perform live retrieval through model-side tools; retrieval happens before the model call and is injected into the prompt. It should use the supplied evidence, ask follow-up questions when knowledge is thin, and avoid making unsupported business assertions. 201 202 WHAT IT IS FED (input) 203 - The Oracle system prompt and conversation rules. 204 - Recent channel messages and the user's latest message. 205 - Deterministically retrieved approved claims, quotes, open gaps, Brain snippets, and metadata when relevant. 206 - Optional image/document attachment text or image parts when the selected route supports vision. 207 - Provider cache hints and, for Qwen, a reusable session handle. 208 Typical topics: process questions, shipment routing, product-development decisions, customer exceptions, designer file practices, system workflows, meeting follow-ups, and "is this still true?" recertification questions. 209 210 WHAT IS EXPECTED (output) 211 - A helpful, grounded chat response to the employee. 212 - Usually one concise answer or one high-value follow-up question. 213 - No invented policy, no fake citations, no claim IDs exposed to the employee unless the UI explicitly asks for them. 214 - Clear distinction between "the approved knowledge says..." and "I do not have enough evidence yet." 215 - Warm, tactful language for people-sensitive or blame-prone topics. 216 - Good behavior in multi-turn context: remember the current conversation, but do not treat recent chat as approved truth unless the prompt labels it that way. 217 218 PROCESS IT USES 219 A synchronous, user-facing text or multimodal generation call. The user is waiting. The model receives prebuilt context blocks and must compose a response directly. It may receive tool-oriented prompt structure, but the direct provider adapters do not run arbitrary Vercel AI SDK tools; choose a model for instruction following over agentic tool autonomy. 220 221 WHAT MAKES A MODEL PERFECTLY SUITED 222 - Low latency under real chat pressure, ideally comfortably under 8 seconds including retrieval and provider overhead. 223 - Excellent instruction following and refusal discipline: grounded answers, one-question behavior, no unsupported business facts. 224 - Strong long-context handling for channel history, retrieved evidence, and document/image attachment context. 225 - Good vision support for product photos, diagrams, screenshots, and uploaded-document images shared in chat. 226 - Tool/function-call capability and structured-output capability are useful because this role shares infrastructure with direct adapters and may be extended with more structured chat behaviors. 227 - Strong conversational tone: clear, calm, tactful, and natural with employees. 228 - Good cache behavior for reusable system and conversation prefixes. 229 - Reasonable cost: this runs on demand whenever employees chat, so cost can climb quickly. 230 231 CAPABILITIES IT NEEDS (hard requirements in the picker) 232 - Tool/function calling. 233 - Structured output or response-format support. 234 - Vision/image input. 235 - Context window greater than 100K tokens. 236 237 CAPABILITIES THAT ARE NICE TO HAVE 238 - Prompt caching, especially for stable system/context prefixes. 239 - Reasoning controls when they do not harm latency. 240 - PDF/file input if the provider route can use it directly, though much document ingestion happens elsewhere. 241 242 CAPABILITIES IT DOES NOT NEED MOST 243 - Very high reasoning budgets for every turn — often too slow and expensive for routine employee chat. 244 - Huge output length — replies should be concise.

Typical topics: process questions, shipment routing, product-development decisions, customer exceptions, designer file practices, system workflows, meeting follow-ups, and "is this still true?" recertification questions. WHAT IS EXPECTED (output) - A helpful, grounded chat response to the employee. - Usually one concise answer or one high-value follow-up question. - No invented policy, no fake citations, no claim IDs exposed to the employee unless the UI explicitly asks for them. - Clear distinction between "the approved knowledge says..." and "I do not have enough evidence yet." - Warm, tactful language for people-sensitive or blame-prone topics. - Good behavior in multi-turn context: remember the current conversation, but do not treat recent chat as approved truth unless the prompt labels it that way. PROCESS IT USES A synchronous, user-facing text or multimodal generation call. The user is waiting. The model receives prebuilt context blocks and must compose a response directly. It may receive tool-oriented prompt structure, but the direct provider adapters do not run arbitrary Vercel AI SDK tools; choose a model for instruction following over agentic tool autonomy. WHAT MAKES A MODEL PERFECTLY SUITED - Low latency under real chat pressure, ideally comfortably under 8 seconds including retrieval and provider overhead. - Excellent instruction following and refusal discipline: grounded answers, one-question behavior, no unsupported business facts. - Strong long-context handling for channel history, retrieved evidence, and document/image attachment context. - Good vision support for product photos, diagrams, screenshots, and uploaded-document images shared in chat. - Tool/function-call capability and structured-output capability are useful because this role shares infrastructure with direct adapters and may be extended with more structured chat behaviors. - Strong conversational tone: clear, calm, tactful, and natural with employees. - Good cache behavior for reusable system and conversation prefixes. - Reasonable cost: this runs on demand whenever employees chat, so cost can climb quickly. CAPABILITIES IT NEEDS (hard requirements in the picker) - Tool/function calling. - Structured output or response-format support. - Vision/image input. - Context window greater than 100K tokens. CAPABILITIES THAT ARE NICE TO HAVE - Prompt caching, especially for stable system/context prefixes. - Reasoning controls when they do not harm latency. - PDF/file input if the provider route can use it directly, though much document ingestion happens elsewhere. CAPABILITIES IT DOES NOT NEED MOST - Very high reasoning budgets for every turn — often too slow and expensive for routine employee chat. - Huge output length — replies should be concise.

1	# Fix Enhancement: Holistic Macro Understanding of Structured / Diagram Sources
2	
3	## About this document (READ THIS FIRST)
4	
5	**Authorship.** The original diagnosis was written by **Codex**. On **2026-07-03**, **Claude** expanded it into this comprehensive version.
6	
7	- Codex's original text is preserved **verbatim** under **§7 "Original diagnosis (Codex)"**. Nothing Codex wrote has been deleted.
8	- Where Claude disagrees with a specific Codex statement, the Codex line is kept and immediately followed by a callout:
9	  > **⚠️ Claude disagrees with this** — <reason> — <what is actually correct>
10	- Everything in **§0–§6 and §8** is new material added by Claude. Claude-added prose is factual and code-verified; where a claim could not be verified from source alone, it is explicitly flagged.
11	
12	**Evidence basis.** This analysis is grounded in:
13	- The most recent full pipeline run of the target document, cached at
14	  `.cache/macro-comparison/9d09fa89-3a46-465e-a98b-837287c9e22a/rerun-20260702T220901Z/`
15	  (document **"Pop Creations Flow 12112025 (1).png"**, id `9d09fa89-3a46-465e-a98b-837287c9e22a`).
16	- The current worker/prompt/schema source in `apps/workers/`, `packages/ai/`, `packages/oracle-engines/`, and `packages/db/`.
17	
18	**~~One honesty caveat~~ — RESOLVED 2026-07-03 (Trigger logs pulled).** The macro-relationship and coverage workers **threw exceptions** — they did not return empty. Codex's "repeated failures" is **confirmed**. The exact errors and their root cause are in **§5 Bug H (the proximate cause) and Bug I**. In short: the `general` auxiliary route resolves to `qwen/qwen3.7-max`, Qwen cannot reliably emit the nested output schema (it omits the required top-level `relationships`/`findings` array), and the single-pick auxiliary slot has no working fallback, so the task hard-fails with `AllCandidatesFailedError`. An earlier worker version additionally failed on a broken cross-source SQL query before the model call.
19	
20	---
21	
22	## 0. What this application is supposed to do, and how
23	
24	### 0.1 The product goal
25	
26	The Oracle is an **evidence-backed knowledge graph** for POP Creations / Spruce Line. For a source like the Pop Creations swimlane flow diagram, the job is **not** to emit a pile of isolated facts. It is to **understand the source holistically**:
27	
28	- the **complete lifecycle** of a licensed product from buyer concept → design → costing → factory sourcing → licensor approval → sampling → PPS audit → production → shipment to the US;
29	- **who owns each stage** (swimlane / role);
30	- the **order and dependencies** between stages;
31	- the **conditional branches** (new vs. existing product; audit pass vs. fail; licensor comment about legal/packaging vs. creative design);
32	- the **approval loops** (licensor revision cycles, re-sampling);
33	- the **systems of record** and data-entry points (DFlow, ColdLion, MasterData, ClickUp, PPT);
34	- and the **coverage gaps** where the diagram shows something the extraction missed.
35	
36	The failure this document exists to fix: **the Oracle currently reads the diagram as ~240 disconnected "A → B" claims and assembles zero of them into a workflow. It sees every tree and no forest.** A user cannot ask "what is the full path of a product?" or "what changes for new vs. existing products?" because nothing in the system holds the assembled process.
37	
38	### 0.2 The non-negotiable trust contract (why this is hard)
39	
40	The Oracle's core guarantee is **quote-level provenance**: every durable atomic claim must cite an **exact verbatim quote** from one persisted `document_chunk` (or one source message). This is enforced deterministically by the quote validator and is **load-bearing** — it is why the Oracle's answers are auditable.
41	
42	Holistic understanding must be added **without breaking that contract**. The rule (from `DECISIONS.md` `D-macro-understanding-boundary`):
43	
44	- **Source outlines / workflow reads are guidance, not evidence.** They can help the extractor interpret structure; they can never be quoted or become a claim by themselves.
45	- **Atomic claims still require exact quotes.**
46	- **Durable macro relationships cite approved *claim IDs*, not raw model interpretation**, and any consumer (Brain, chat, MCP) must verify every support claim is `approved` **at read time**.
47	
48	### 0.3 How the pipeline is *supposed* to work today
49	
50	```
51	image upload
52	  → vision transcription (Pass 1): image → faithful text topology
53	        nodes  [Shape/Color: "label"]
54	        edges  [A] --(condition)--> [B]
55	        swimlanes ### headers
56	  → structure-aware chunking → document_chunks (persisted, embedded)
57	  → source-outline worker: reads chunks, produces a provisional macro read
58	        (source_outlines + source_groups + recommended lenses)
59	  → document-ingestion broad extraction pass: chunk → extract → quote-validate → promote
60	  → budgeted lens fan-out: document-lens-extraction × N (per source group × lens)
61	  → macro-relationship-extraction: infer cross-claim relationships from validated claims
62	  → source-coverage-audit: compare outline expectations vs. what got extracted
63	  → Brain / chat consume APPROVED claims + APPROVED macro relationships
64	```
65	
66	### 0.4 The one-sentence thesis of the fix
67	
68	**The diagram already encodes the macro structure (sequence, branches, loops, ownership, handoffs). The vision pass captures it. The pipeline then destroys it at the "flatten to atomic claims" step, and asks the macro layer to *rediscover* it from a truncated bag of prose. Stop rediscovering — capture the graph once, persist it as a first-class artifact, derive claims from it, and generate macro relationships from its structure.**
69	
70	---
71	
72	## 1. Observed behavior (the concrete run)
73	
74	Run: `document 9d09fa89-…`, cached rerun `rerun-20260702T220901Z` (Trigger run set from 2026-07-03T02:16Z).
75	
76	**Jobs that ran (from `job-outputs-after-rerun.json`):**
77	
78	| Job | Status | Output |
79	|---|---|---|
80	| `document-ingestion` (broad pass) | complete | 3 chunks, 53 staged, 53 promoted |
81	| `source-outline` | complete | 10 groups, 30 group items, lens fanout `triggered: 4`, `macroFollowups: { triggered: true }` |
82	| `document-lens-extraction` × 4 | complete | each ~52–53 staged, ~46–48 promoted |
83	| `macro-relationship-extraction` | **absent from job trace** | — |
84	| `source-coverage-audit` | **absent from job trace** | — |
85	
86	**Final numbers (from `comparison-summary.md`):**
87	
88	- Old GUI baseline claims: **122**. New post-rerun claims: **241** (net **+119**).
89	- New claims came from only **56 unique evidence quotes** (old: 66). → ≈**4× duplication**.
90	- **`macro_relationships` for the document: 0.**
91	- **`source_coverage_findings` for the document: 0.**
92	- Only **6** of 241 claims auto-approved; the rest are `pending_review`.
93	
94	**Lens fan-out coverage (from the outline job's `lensFanout.skipped` list):**
95	
96	- The 4 lens calls that ran were **all the `handoffs` lens**, on groups sorted 0–3 (Buyer Engagement, Creative Direction, Design Execution, Costing).
97	- Groups sorted 8–9 — **"SKU Creation & System Entry"** and **"Mass Production & Shipment"** — were dropped entirely (`group_skipped_over_max_8`).
98	- Every other lens (exceptions, dependencies, systems, ownership, risk) on every group was skipped (`model_call_count_exceeds_4`).
99	
100	**Vision transcription quality (from the claim quotes):** the transcription is **excellent** — every branch, loop, and terminal stage in the diagram appears *somewhere* as an isolated edge quote (`If Audit: Pass`/`If Audit: Fail`, `New Products`/`existing products`, licensor legal-vs-creative branches, `Ship to US`, etc.). The raw material for holistic understanding was captured. It was simply never assembled.
101	
102	---
103	
104	## 2. Errors & shortcomings in the Oracle's analysis
105	
106	### 2.1 The workflow, as it actually is (Claude's holistic read, reconstructed from the Oracle's own transcription)
107

const MACRO_MODEL_BRIEF = `THE ORACLE — "Macro Understanding Model" role brief
(Paste this into a model-evaluation assistant or vendor comparison to judge whether a given model is the right pick for this setting.)

PURPOSE
This model builds The Oracle's HOLISTIC understanding of a source. Atomic extraction (a different model) breaks a document into many small, quote-validated "claims." That is necessary but not sufficient: a swimlane diagram or SOP is a whole business process, not a bag of isolated arrows. This model's job is to understand and represent the BIG PICTURE — the end-to-end lifecycle, who owns each stage, the order and dependencies, the conditional branches, the approval loops, the systems of record, and what the source shows that the atomic claims failed to capture. If atomic extraction answers "what are the facts," this model answers "what is the process, and does our set of facts faithfully represent it."

WHERE IT SITS IN THE PIPELINE (it runs both BEFORE and AFTER atomic extraction)
Three background (Trigger.dev) workers share this one model setting:
  1. source-outline — runs on/near ingestion. Reads the whole source (all chunks, and for images the transcription) and produces a provisional structured "outline": the source's purpose, process stages, actors/owners, systems, customers/licensors, geographies, term/acronym definitions, handoffs, exceptions, open questions, recommended extraction "lenses," and meaning-based groupings of the source. This is the BEFORE pass: its output is injected as non-quotable CONTEXT so atomic extraction understands the source's shape (referents, acronyms, branches) instead of extracting blind. The outline is GUIDANCE ONLY — never evidence, never quotable.
  2. macro-relationship-extraction — runs AFTER atomic claims exist. Reads the already-validated claims and proposes durable cross-claim relationships (dependency, handoff, sequence, exception_path, policy_vs_practice_tension, workaround_to_system_limitation, definition_resolution). Each relationship must be supported by ≥2 real claim IDs — its evidence is claim IDs, NOT free text.
  3. source-coverage-audit — runs AFTER. Compares the outline's expectations against the claims + relationships actually produced and emits "coverage findings" (missing stage, missing owner, missing branch, unresolved reference, unrepresented exception, low_claim_density, macro_only_source). Findings become gaps; they never assert new facts as true.

WHAT IT IS FED (input)
- A large STRUCTURED-JSON system prompt + schema for whichever of the three tasks is running.
- For outlines: the full source text/topology (can be long — a whole document or diagram transcription), plus, for visual sources, the image itself when the model is multimodal.
- For relationships: a bounded set of candidate claim summaries with their IDs, types, "claim kinds," and statuses.
- For coverage: the outline JSON plus the extracted claims/relationships JSON.
It is NOT user-facing. Nobody is waiting on it. It runs asynchronously in the background at document scale.

WHAT IS EXPECTED (output) — this is the crux
- DEEP, NESTED, SCHEMA-VALID JSON, every time. The output schemas are large and nested (arrays of stages, each with arrays of inputs/outputs/nextStageIds/sourceRefs; arrays of relationships each with an array of supportingClaims with roles; arrays of findings). The runtime validates the output against a strict Zod schema and FAILS THE WHOLE TASK if a required field or array is missing.
- Faithful, conservative reasoning: say "unknown" when the source doesn't say; never invent stages, owners, or entities; only reference claim IDs / source refs from the supplied list; never introduce a named entity not present in the support claims or the entity registry.
- Genuine holistic comprehension of process structure: correctly reading a diagram's sequence, branches (new vs existing, pass vs fail), loops (revision/re-sampling), cross-swimlane handoffs, and terminal outcomes.

PROCESS IT USES
Asynchronous, background, single-shot STRUCTURED-OUTPUT generation (runObject) per task. Long input, long nested output. No human latency requirement. It reasons over structure; it does not fetch anything itself (retrieval/claim-selection happens before the call).

WHAT MAKES A MODEL PERFECTLY SUITED
- NATIVE STRICT JSON-SCHEMA structured output (Gemini responseJsonSchema, OpenAI json_schema strict). This is the single most important attribute — see hard requirements.
- Strong multi-step reasoning over structure: assembling a lifecycle, detecting branches/loops/handoffs, spotting coverage gaps and policy-vs-practice tension. This is a synthesis/reasoning task, closer to the Synthesis role than to verbatim extraction.
- Large context window: outlines can ingest a whole document or a dense diagram transcription in one call (aim comfortably >100K, more is better).
- Generous max OUTPUT tokens: the nested JSON for a 10-stage workflow with branches is large; truncated output = schema failure.
- Multimodal (image) input is a strong PLUS for source outlines of diagrams/flowcharts/spec sheets, so the model can reconcile the picture with the parsed text.
- Reliability/determinism of schema adherence across retries. Cost/throughput matter (runs at document volume, async), but correctness of structured output dominates.

CAPABILITIES IT NEEDS (hard requirements)
- STRICT structured output / JSON-schema mode — NON-NEGOTIABLE. A model that only offers loose "json_object" (return-some-JSON) is NOT acceptable: it intermittently omits required arrays and the task hard-fails with AllCandidatesFailedError. (This exact failure took the macro layer to zero on the Pop Creations flow diagram when this slot pointed at Qwen qwen3.7-max.)
- Large context window (>100K; bigger is better for whole-source outlines).
- High max-output-token ceiling for deep nested JSON.
- Strong instruction-following and reasoning.

CAPABILITIES THAT ARE NICE TO HAVE
- Image/multimodal input (helps outline quality on visual sources).
- Reasoning/"thinking" controls — modest effort can help assemble structure; keep an eye on cost.
- Prompt caching for the stable schema/system prefix.

CAPABILITIES IT DOES NOT NEED
- Low latency / streaming — it is a background job; nobody is watching.
- Tool / function calling — retrieval and claim selection happen before the call.
- Verbatim-quote fidelity — unlike atomic extraction, this model does not quote the source; its evidence is claim IDs and its outline is explicitly non-quotable.

NEGATIVE ATTRIBUTES (avoid)
- Loose-JSON-only providers (json_object without strict schema) — the top cause of failure here.
- Small context or small max-output models — they truncate the nested JSON and fail validation.
- Models that hallucinate entities/stages or ignore "reference only the supplied IDs" instructions — they poison provenance and get rejected by the entity/support validators anyway.

BOTTOM LINE
Pick a strong long-context REASONING model with NATIVE STRICT JSON-SCHEMA output and a high output-token ceiling; multimodal is a bonus for diagrams. Do NOT pick a loose-json-object-only model, and do NOT optimize for latency. This slot is intentionally separate from the Extraction model: extraction mines verbatim-quoted facts; this model reasons about the whole process and must emit deep nested schema-valid JSON.`;

// Clipboard "job brief" text by auxiliary-model id. Declared after the brief
// literals (which are large) so the earlier AUX_PRESENTATION map stays free of
// forward references. Aux models without a brief simply omit an entry.
const AUX_CLIPBOARD_BRIEFS: Record<string, string> = {
  vision: VISION_MODEL_BRIEF,
  macro: MACRO_MODEL_BRIEF,
  translation: TRANSLATION_MODEL_BRIEF,
};

lines: BOTTOM LINE

Pick the model with the best, most natural translation quality for your exact language pair and the strictest "output only the translation" discipline — a Chinese-native flagship (e.g. Qwen-Max-tier) is a strong default for English<->Chinese. content.text: **5) Migration + seed for `default_macro_route` (defaulting to a strict-schema model, not Qwen):**

input.content:
-- Macro-understanding model route.
--
-- The source-outline, macro-relationship-extraction, and source-coverage-audit
-- workers previously borrowed the `default_general_purpose_route` (utility)
-- slot. That slot pointed at qwen/qwen3.7-max, which only supports loose
-- `json_object` output (not strict JSON schema) and intermittently omits the
-- required top-level arrays, hard-failing the macro layer with
-- AllCandidatesFailedError (see AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md). The macro layer now has its
-- own explicit, admin-visible slot that requires strict structured output.
--
-- Default seeded to google/gemini-2.5-flash: native responseJsonSchema, large
-- context, cheap, and already the proven production extraction model. Admins can
-- change it at Admin -> Settings -> "Macro understanding model" with no redeploy.
-- Idempotent: never clobbers an admin's existing choice.

BEGIN;

INSERT INTO settings (key, value, description)
VALUES
  (
    'default_macro_route',
    '"google/gemini-2.5-flash"'::jsonb,
    'Model for the macro/holistic layer (source outlines, macro relationship extraction, coverage audits). Requires strict structured-output support; do not point at a json_object-only model such as Qwen.'
  )
ON CONFLICT (key) DO NOTHING;

COMMIT;

Now **Part B — the failure log for AI agents.** Creating the designated file, seeded with the verified failures, in an AI-actionable format:

input.content:
# AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md — runtime failures for a coding agent to fix

## What this file is (read first)

This is the **designated place where runtime failures are written so a coding AI agent (or human) can act on them.** The Oracle's background workers run on Trigger.dev and cannot write into this repo, so a failure that only lands in Trigger.dev's dashboard or a `model_run_attempts` row is, in practice, **failing silently** — nobody and no agent is watching those. This file is the durable, in-repo, agent-readable digest.

**Hard rule (design phase):** if a worker/run fails, it must end up here, in a form specific enough to change code from — exact error signature, where it surfaced, root cause with `file:line`, evidence (run IDs / DB counts), the fix, and status. `HANDOFF.md` links here so every session sees it.

### How entries get here (until fully automated)

1. **Now (manual/session):** when a run is found failed (Trigger MCP `list_runs`/`get_run_details`, or DB `job_runs`/`model_run_attempts`), append/refresh an entry below.
2. **Required enabling work (see fix_enhancement.md §5 Bug C / priority 1):** the macro/coverage/outline workers must write `job_runs` rows and a document-level `macro_health` status. Once they do, `scripts/export-worker-failures.mjs` (to be written) can query recent failed `job_runs` + `model_run_attempts` and refresh the "Open failures" section automatically. **Until that lands, absence of an entry here does NOT mean absence of failure — check Trigger.dev and DB counts too.**

### Entry status legend

- **OPEN** — still failing in the current deployed worker.
- **MITIGATED** — a code/config change addresses it but is not yet verified in prod (needs deploy/migration/re-run).
- **FIXED** — verified not failing after the change (record the evidence).

---

## Open / recent failures

### ERR-001 — Macro & coverage workers hard-fail: `AllCandidatesFailedError` (Qwen can't emit strict schema, no fallback)  — MITIGATED (pending deploy + migration + re-run)

- **Symptom:** `macro-relationship-extraction` and `source-coverage-audit` **failed on every run** for document `9d09fa89-3a46-465e-a98b-837287c9e22a`, so the holistic layer produced `macro_relationships = 0` and `source_coverage_findings = 0` while the document still showed `complete`.
- **Where it surfaced:** Trigger.dev prod, project `proj_wgpzsvhmsopqhvwqaycn`, worker `20260703.2`. Evidence runs: `run_cmr4bxmwi64y00un5c6a7nne0` (macro), `run_cmr4bxqat68b40uof9kxb3bts` (coverage). Both retried 3× and failed identically (~3.7–4.6m, ~$0.008).
- **Exact error:**
  ```
  AllCandidatesFailedError: All model candidates failed for general:
    qwen_3_7_max_extraction_eval (qwen/qwen3.7-max):
    [{ "code":"invalid_type", "path":["relationships"], "message":"expected array, received undefined" }]
  ```
  (coverage identical at `path: ["findings", 0, …]`).
- **Root cause:**
  1. Both workers resolved the model via the **`general` auxiliary slot** (`resolveRouteCandidates(db, 'general')`), which in prod pointed at **`qwen/qwen3.7-max`**. NOTE: this is a *separate* setting from the Extraction and Vision model pickers — changing those in Admin → Settings did **not** change what the macro layer used.
  2. Qwen uses `response_format: json_object` + Zod (**not** strict json-schema; see `DECISIONS.md` D12 / `docs/architecture.md`). On the deep nested `MacroRelationshipOutputSchema` / `CoverageAuditOutputSchema` it omits the required top-level array → Zod `invalid_type`.
  3. The `general` slot is **single-pick with no working fallback** (`PROVIDER UNAVAILABLE: "deepseek" ... DEEPSEEK_API_KEY is not set`), so one malformed response fails the whole task.
- **Fix applied (this change, 2026-07-03):**
  - Added a dedicated **`macro` auxiliary slot** with its own admin picker `default_macro_route` (Admin → Settings → "Macro understanding model"), a `requiredCapability: 'structuredOutputs'` filter, and a "Copy job brief" that spells out strict-json-schema as a hard requirement.
    - `packages/ai/src/routes/{errors,defaults,auxiliary,capability-requirements}.ts`, `apps/web/app/admin/settings/page.tsx`.
  - Repointed `source-outline`, `macro-relationship-extraction`, `source-coverage-audit` from `'general'` → `'macro'`.
  - Seeded `default_macro_route = google/gemini-2.5-flash` (native `responseJsonSchema`, proven prod model): `packages/db/migrations/sql/83_macro_route_setting.sql` + `packages/db/src/seed.ts`.
- **Still OPEN (do next):**
  - The `macro` slot is still single-pick — add a **fallback pool** and/or a **schema-repair sub-pass** so one bad structured-output response can't zero the layer. (`AllCandidatesFailedError` should be survivable, not fatal, for a background enrichment task.)
  - Set `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` in the worker env or stop advertising deepseek as a phantom fallback.
- **Verification needed:** apply migration 83 to prod, deploy workers, re-run document `9d09fa89`, confirm `macro_relationships > 0` and `source_coverage_findings ≥ 0` with a successful macro/coverage `model_runs` row on provider `google`. Then flip this to FIXED with the run IDs.

### ERR-002 — Cross-source support-claims SQL query failed before the model  — LIKELY FIXED (confirm)

- **Symptom:** `macro-relationship-extraction` failed fast (~4.5s, $0.0002, before any model call) on worker `20260703.1`.
- **Evidence run:** `run_cmr4bvh2269i90jn94k5e17s2` (and siblings), payload `{ documentId: 9d09fa89…, relationshipScope: "cross_source" }`.
- **Exact error:** `Error: Failed query:` on the `cross_source` CTE (`seed_claims` / `seed_domains` / `related_claims`) in `loadSupportClaims` — `apps/workers/src/trigger/macro-relationship-extraction.ts:146`.
- **Status:** worker `20260703.2` reached the model (see ERR-001), implying the query was fixed between `.1` and `.2`. **Not independently verified.**
- **Do next:** confirm the current `20260703.2` CTE executes against prod for a `cross_source` payload; add a smoke test that runs it against a seeded fixture so it can't silently regress.

---

## Systemic observability gaps that let the above hide (fix so failures can't be silent again)

These are tracked in detail in `fix_enhancement.md` §5 (Bugs C, D, G) and its fix priority. Summarized here because they are the reason ERR-001 went unseen:

- **Macro/coverage/outline workers do not write `job_runs` rows** → they never appear in the Oracle's own "AI runs" dashboard; "failed 12×" looks identical to "never ran". **Fix:** write `job_runs` (running → failed/complete) like every other worker.
- **No document-level `macro_health`** → a document with a totally failed holistic layer still displays `status = complete` (green). **Fix:** add `macro_health` (`pending | complete | degraded | failed`), surface it in Admin → Documents, and set it from the followup outcomes.
- **Followups are fire-and-forget** (`source-outline.ts` `.trigger(...).catch(warn)`) → parent job succeeds regardless. **Fix:** propagate failure into `macro_health`, and sequence coverage *after* macro (Bug G).

---

## Change log

- **2026-07-03:** File created. Seeded ERR-001 (macro/coverage Qwen schema hard-fail — mitigated by the dedicated `macro` route) and ERR-002 (cross-source SQL failure). Documented the observability gaps that hid them. Referenced from `HANDOFF.md`.

These are tracked in detail in `fix_enhancement.md` §5 (Bugs C, D, G) and its fix priority. Summarized here because they are the reason ERR-001 went unseen:

- **Macro/coverage/outline workers do not write `job_runs` rows** → they never appear in the Oracle's own "AI runs" dashboard; "failed 12×" looks identical to "never ran". **Fix:** write `job_runs` (running → failed/complete) like every other worker.
- **No document-level `macro_health`** → a document with a totally failed holistic layer still displays `status = complete` (green). **Fix:** add `macro_health` (`pending | complete | degraded | failed`), surface it in Admin → Documents, and set it from the followup outcomes.
- **Followups are fire-and-forget** (`source-outline.ts` `.trigger(...).catch(warn)`) → parent job succeeds regardless. **Fix:** propagate failure into `macro_health`, and sequence coverage *after* macro (Bug G).

---

## Change log

- **2026-07-03:** File created. Seeded ERR-001 (macro/coverage Qwen schema hard-fail — mitigated by the dedicated `macro` route) and ERR-002 (cross-source SQL failure). Documented the observability gaps that hid them. Referenced from `HANDOFF.md`.

input.new_string:
## 6.9 Changes implemented 2026-07-03, and the before/after ordering decision (Claude)

### Implemented this session

1. **Dedicated `macro` model slot (fixes the ERR-001 proximate cause).** The macro layer no longer borrows the `general` utility slot. New auxiliary slot `macro` with its own admin picker **`default_macro_route`** (Admin → Settings → "Macro understanding model"), a `requiredCapability: 'structuredOutputs'` filter, and a comprehensive **"Copy job brief"** (spells out that STRICT json-schema — not loose `json_object` — is a hard requirement, that it runs before *and* after atomic extraction, and what capabilities help/hurt). Seeded to `google/gemini-2.5-flash`.
   - Files: `packages/ai/src/routes/{errors,defaults,auxiliary,capability-requirements}.ts`; `apps/web/app/admin/settings/page.tsx`; `packages/db/migrations/sql/83_macro_route_setting.sql`; `packages/db/src/seed.ts`; workers `source-outline.ts`, `macro-relationship-extraction.ts`, `source-coverage-audit.ts` (`'general'` → `'macro'`, incl. attempt-logging slot).
   - **Why a separate slot at all:** macro is a reasoning/synthesis task over claims + structure, not verbatim quote-extraction — but the real point is it must be a *visible, explicit* choice, not an anonymous inheritance from "utility." See the settings job brief.
   - **Needs to go live:** apply migration 83, deploy workers, re-run doc `9d09fa89`, confirm `macro_relationships > 0`. Tracked as `AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md` ERR-001.
   - **Still open:** the slot is single-pick — add a **fallback pool + schema-repair pass** so one malformed structured-output response can't zero the layer.

2. **`AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md`** — a designated, in-repo, agent-readable failure log, referenced from `HANDOFF.md`. Seeded with ERR-001 (Qwen schema hard-fail) and ERR-002 (cross-source SQL). Rule: runtime failures must be captured here in a form specific enough to change code from — because a failure that lives only in Trigger.dev/`model_run_attempts` and nobody reads *is* a silent failure. Enabling work still open: macro/coverage must write `job_runs` + a `macro_health` status, then a `scripts/export-worker-failures.mjs` can refresh the log automatically.

### The "macro before AND after atomic extraction" requirement — current state is WRONG, must change

Intended design (agreed): a macro read **before** atomic extraction (so extraction has whole-source context — referents, acronyms, branches, ownership) **and** a macro pass **after** (relationships + coverage, to check the claims reflect the big picture).

**Verified current reality — the "before" is not actually happening:**
- In `document-ingestion.ts`, `processDocument` runs parse → chunk → embed → **broad atomic extraction** (`buildDocumentChunkWindows`, ~line 713) → promote → mark document `complete` (~line 1291), and only THEN triggers `source-outline` (~line 1298). So the outline for a document is generated **after** its atomic extraction already ran.
- The broad extraction *does* have an outline-injection point (`source-outline-guidance` block, ~line 777), but it injects only a **prior** outline and only when `macro_outline_injection_enabled = true` — which is **seeded `false`** (migration 79). On a first ingestion there is no prior outline, so the broad pass runs blind.
- Net: today it's effectively **after-only**. The outline (the "before" artifact) exists but runs too late to inform the extraction it was meant to guide, and injection is off.

**Target change (open, not yet implemented — larger architectural edit):**
1. Reorder `processDocument`: generate the source outline **immediately after chunk+embed, before** `buildDocumentChunkWindows` extraction (await it; it's one model call).
2. Inject that fresh outline into the broad extraction as non-quotable guidance, and turn `macro_outline_injection_enabled` on (after a small pilot).
3. Keep `macro-relationship-extraction` + `source-coverage-audit` as the **after** pass, and sequence coverage *after* macro (Bug G).
4. This composes with the workflow-map artifact (§7): ideally the "before" pass produces the workflow map, extraction is driven from it, and the "after" pass derives relationships from it.

This is a deliberate follow-up because it changes prod extraction ordering and cost; it should ship with the `macro_health` observability so a failed/blind pass is visible.

---

## 7. Original diagnosis (Codex) — preserved verbatim

content.text:
All implemented and typechecked. Here's the summary.

## What I built

**1) A dedicated, clearly-labeled macro picker on the settings page** — Admin → Settings now has a **"Macro understanding model"** card (between Vision and General-purpose), backed by its own setting `default_macro_route`. It has the same **"Copy job brief"** button as the others (it appears automatically because the brief is registered). The brief is comprehensive: purpose, that it runs **before *and* after** atomic extraction, the three workers it drives, the exact output schemas, what makes a model suited, the **hard requirement of strict JSON-schema** (with an explicit warning that loose `json_object`-only models like Qwen will hard-fail), context/output-token needs, multimodal-as-a-plus, and the negative attributes to avoid.

The macro workers (`source-outline`, `macro-relationship-extraction`, `source-coverage-audit`) are repointed from the shared `general` slot to the new `macro` slot, and the capability filter enforces `structuredOutputs`. Seeded default: **`google/gemini-2.5-flash`** (native `responseJsonSchema`, not Qwen).

Files: `packages/ai/src/routes/{errors,defaults,auxiliary,capability-requirements}.ts`, `apps/web/app/admin/settings/page.tsx`, the 3 workers, `packages/db/migrations/sql/83_macro_route_setting.sql`, `packages/db/src/seed.ts`. **`@oracle/ai`, `@oracle/workers`, `@oracle/db` typecheck clean.** (The web typecheck has pre-existing `@oracle/engines` module-resolution errors in files I didn't touch — I confirmed via a stash test that they exist without my changes; it's a missing workspace symlink locally. My `settings/page.tsx` change never appears in the errors.)

**Why a different model at all (your question):** it shouldn't be a *hidden* different model. Macro is genuinely a reasoning/synthesis task over claims + structure (closer to Synthesis than to verbatim extraction), so a distinct choice is defensible — but the real fix is making it a **visible, explicit** picker instead of silently inheriting the anonymous "utility" slot. That's now done.

**2) Failures visible to the AI, not just logged into the void** — created **`AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md`**: a designated, in-repo, agent-readable failure log, seeded with ERR-001 (the Qwen schema hard-fail) and ERR-002 (the cross-source SQL failure), each with exact error, `file:line` root cause, run IDs, fix, and status. **Referenced prominently in `HANDOFF.md`.** The doc states the rule plainly: a failure that lives only in Trigger.dev is a silent failure.

**3) Your "before AND after" recollection — you're right, and it's currently NOT that way.** Verified: `document-ingestion` runs the broad **atomic extraction first**, marks the document `complete`, and only *then* triggers `source-outline` (line ~1298). And outline injection into extraction is **seeded off** (`macro_outline_injection_enabled=false`). So today it's effectively **after-only** — the "before" outline exists but runs too late to inform the extraction it was meant to guide. I recorded the target (generate the outline *before* extraction, inject it, keep relationships/coverage as the after pass) in `fix_enhancement.md` §6.9 as an open architectural change.

## What still has to happen (I did not deploy or commit)

To make ERR-001 actually go green:
1. Apply migration `83_macro_route_setting.sql` to prod.
2. Deploy the workers.
3. Re-run document `9d09fa89`, confirm `macro_relationships > 0` with a successful `provider=google` macro run.

Still open (I can take these next, in this order):
- **Fallback + schema-repair for the macro slot** — right now it's still single-pick, so one bad response can still zero the layer. This is the "stop failing silently → make it survivable" piece.
- **`job_runs` + document-level `macro_health`** — so a failed holistic layer can never show as a green `complete` document, and so the failure log can auto-populate.
- **The before/after reorder** of `document-ingestion` (the Part-C change).

Want me to proceed with the fallback + `macro_health` observability next (the two that most directly enforce "failures can't hide"), and/or verify the live `default_general_purpose_route` value and apply/deploy the macro-route change?

content.text:
Key finding: `source-outline` **already writes `job_runs`** — only `macro-relationship-extraction` and `source-coverage-audit` don't. And auxiliary slots can get a fallback pool for free via the existing pool machinery. That de-risks the plan. Here's the order I'll execute (safest/highest-value first), typechecking at the end:

1. Lens budget → coverage-first (fixes terminal-stage skip) + Bug C idempotency
2. Bug E (support-claim `LIMIT`)
3. Macro slot fallback pool (`model_pool_macro`) for resilience
4. Observability: `documents.macro_health` + `job_runs` in macro/coverage workers, race-tolerant health precedence
5. Migrations + seed, typecheck, docs

Let me start with the lens budget rewrite.

input.content:
-- Macro fallback pool + coverage-first lens budgets (2026-07-03).
--
-- (1) model_pool_macro: the macro slot now has an ordered fallback chain so one
--     malformed structured-output response can't zero the holistic layer
--     (AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md ERR-001). Must contain the default_macro_route primary
--     (google/gemini-2.5-flash) and only strict-json-schema-capable models.
--
-- (2) Lens fan-out budgets: the old defaults (model_calls=4, groups=8) plus the
--     lens-major selection meant a 10-stage swimlane diagram ran ONE lens on the
--     first 4 stages and dropped the terminal stages entirely (fix_enhancement.md
--     §5 Bugs A/B/F). The selection is now coverage-first (every stage gets its
--     top lens before any stage gets a second), so the model-call ceiling must be
--     high enough to cover the stages. These are UPDATEs (not inserts) because the
--     rows already exist from migration 81; this is a deliberate behavior fix.

BEGIN;

INSERT INTO settings (key, value, description)
VALUES
  (
    'model_pool_macro',
    '["google/gemini-2.5-flash", "google/gemini-2.5-pro", "openai/gpt-4.1-mini"]'::jsonb,
    'Ordered fallback chain for the macro slot (source outlines, macro relationships, coverage audits). Must include the default_macro_route primary and only strict-structured-output models.'
  )
ON CONFLICT (key) DO NOTHING;

-- Coverage-adequate lens fan-out ceilings. Cost stays bounded by the token
-- ceiling and the model-call cap; the selection covers stages breadth-first.
UPDATE settings SET value = '16'::jsonb
  WHERE key = 'macro_max_lens_model_calls_per_document';
UPDATE settings SET value = '20'::jsonb
  WHERE key = 'macro_max_lens_groups_per_document';
UPDATE settings SET value = '64000'::jsonb
  WHERE key = 'macro_max_lens_estimated_input_tokens';

COMMIT;

input.content:
-- documents.macro_health (2026-07-03).
--
-- Document-level health of the macro (holistic) layer, so a document whose macro
-- layer failed can never read as a green `status = complete`. Written by the
-- source-outline / macro-relationship-extraction / source-coverage-audit workers
-- (apps/workers/src/lib/macro-health.ts). Added via hand-written SQL (like
-- documents.context / domain_hints, migration 65) plus schema.ts; a generated
-- Drizzle migration does not accompany it, so db:check-drift may flag it.
--
-- Values: not_applicable | pending | complete | degraded | failed.
--   not_applicable — no macro layer attempted (e.g. non-document sources).
--   pending        — outline dispatched the followups; awaiting their outcome.
--   complete       — a followup succeeded and nothing failed.
--   degraded       — a macro/coverage followup failed (partial holistic layer).
--   failed         — the outline itself failed (no holistic layer at all).

BEGIN;

ALTER TABLE documents
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS macro_health varchar(20) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'not_applicable';

DO $$
BEGIN
  IF NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM pg_constraint WHERE conname = 'documents_macro_health_check'
  ) THEN
    ALTER TABLE documents
      ADD CONSTRAINT documents_macro_health_check
      CHECK (macro_health IN ('not_applicable', 'pending', 'complete', 'degraded', 'failed'));
  END IF;
END$$;

COMMIT;

# AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md — runtime failures for a coding agent to fix

## What this file is (read first)

This is the **designated place where runtime failures are written so a coding AI agent (or human) can act on them.** The Oracle's background workers run on Trigger.dev and cannot write into this repo, so a failure that only lands in Trigger.dev's dashboard or a `model_run_attempts` row is, in practice, **failing silently** — nobody and no agent is watching those. This file is the durable, in-repo, agent-readable digest.

**Hard rule (design phase):** if a worker/run fails, it must end up here, in a form specific enough to change code from — exact error signature, where it surfaced, root cause with `file:line`, evidence (run IDs / DB counts), the fix, and status. `HANDOFF.md` links here so every session sees it.

### How entries get here (until fully automated)

1. **Now (manual/session):** when a run is found failed (Trigger MCP `list_runs`/`get_run_details`, or DB `job_runs`/`model_run_attempts`), append/refresh an entry below.
2. **Required enabling work (see fix_enhancement.md §5 Bug C / priority 1):** the macro/coverage/outline workers must write `job_runs` rows and a document-level `macro_health` status. Once they do, `scripts/export-worker-failures.mjs` (to be written) can query recent failed `job_runs` + `model_run_attempts` and refresh the "Open failures" section automatically. **Until that lands, absence of an entry here does NOT mean absence of failure — check Trigger.dev and DB counts too.**

### Entry status legend

- **OPEN** — still failing in the current deployed worker.
- **MITIGATED** — a code/config change addresses it but is not yet verified in prod (needs deploy/migration/re-run).
- **FIXED** — verified not failing after the change (record the evidence).

---

## Open / recent failures

### ERR-001 — Macro & coverage workers hard-fail: `AllCandidatesFailedError` (Qwen can't emit strict schema, no fallback)  — MITIGATED (pending deploy + migration + re-run)

- **Symptom:** `macro-relationship-extraction` and `source-coverage-audit` **failed on every run** for document `9d09fa89-3a46-465e-a98b-837287c9e22a`, so the holistic layer produced `macro_relationships = 0` and `source_coverage_findings = 0` while the document still showed `complete`.
- **Where it surfaced:** Trigger.dev prod, project `proj_wgpzsvhmsopqhvwqaycn`, worker `20260703.2`. Evidence runs: `run_cmr4bxmwi64y00un5c6a7nne0` (macro), `run_cmr4bxqat68b40uof9kxb3bts` (coverage). Both retried 3× and failed identically (~3.7–4.6m, ~$0.008).
- **Exact error:**
  ```
  AllCandidatesFailedError: All model candidates failed for general:
    qwen_3_7_max_extraction_eval (qwen/qwen3.7-max):
    [{ "code":"invalid_type", "path":["relationships"], "message":"expected array, received undefined" }]
  ```
  (coverage identical at `path: ["findings", 0, …]`).
- **Root cause:**
  1. Both workers resolved the model via the **`general` auxiliary slot** (`resolveRouteCandidates(db, 'general')`), which in prod pointed at **`qwen/qwen3.7-max`**. NOTE: this is a *separate* setting from the Extraction and Vision model pickers — changing those in Admin → Settings did **not** change what the macro layer used.
  2. Qwen uses `response_format: json_object` + Zod (**not** strict json-schema; see `DECISIONS.md` D12 / `docs/architecture.md`). On the deep nested `MacroRelationshipOutputSchema` / `CoverageAuditOutputSchema` it omits the required top-level array → Zod `invalid_type`.
  3. The `general` slot is **single-pick with no working fallback** (`PROVIDER UNAVAILABLE: "deepseek" ... DEEPSEEK_API_KEY is not set`), so one malformed response fails the whole task.
- **Fix applied (this change, 2026-07-03):**
  - Added a dedicated **`macro` auxiliary slot** with its own admin picker `default_macro_route` (Admin → Settings → "Macro understanding model"), a `requiredCapability: 'structuredOutputs'` filter, and a "Copy job brief" that spells out strict-json-schema as a hard requirement.
    - `packages/ai/src/routes/{errors,defaults,auxiliary,capability-requirements}.ts`, `apps/web/app/admin/settings/page.tsx`.
  - Repointed `source-outline`, `macro-relationship-extraction`, `source-coverage-audit` from `'general'` → `'macro'`.
  - Seeded `default_macro_route = google/gemini-2.5-flash` (native `responseJsonSchema`, proven prod model): `packages/db/migrations/sql/83_macro_route_setting.sql` + `packages/db/src/seed.ts`.
- **Still OPEN (do next):**
  - The `macro` slot is still single-pick — add a **fallback pool** and/or a **schema-repair sub-pass** so one bad structured-output response can't zero the layer. (`AllCandidatesFailedError` should be survivable, not fatal, for a background enrichment task.)
  - Set `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` in the worker env or stop advertising deepseek as a phantom fallback.
- **Verification needed:** apply migration 83 to prod, deploy workers, re-run document `9d09fa89`, confirm `macro_relationships > 0` and `source_coverage_findings ≥ 0` with a successful macro/coverage `model_runs` row on provider `google`. Then flip this to FIXED with the run IDs.

### ERR-002 — Cross-source support-claims SQL query failed before the model  — LIKELY FIXED (confirm)

- **Symptom:** `macro-relationship-extraction` failed fast (~4.5s, $0.0002, before any model call) on worker `20260703.1`.
- **Evidence run:** `run_cmr4bvh2269i90jn94k5e17s2` (and siblings), payload `{ documentId: 9d09fa89…, relationshipScope: "cross_source" }`.
- **Exact error:** `Error: Failed query:` on the `cross_source` CTE (`seed_claims` / `seed_domains` / `related_claims`) in `loadSupportClaims` — `apps/workers/src/trigger/macro-relationship-extraction.ts:146`.
- **Status:** worker `20260703.2` reached the model (see ERR-001), implying the query was fixed between `.1` and `.2`. **Not independently verified.**
- **Do next:** confirm the current `20260703.2` CTE executes against prod for a `cross_source` payload; add a smoke test that runs it against a seeded fixture so it can't silently regress.

---

## Systemic observability gaps that let the above hide (fix so failures can't be silent again)

These are tracked in detail in `fix_enhancement.md` §5 (Bugs C, D, G) and its fix priority. Summarized here because they are the reason ERR-001 went unseen:

- **Macro/coverage/outline workers do not write `job_runs` rows** → they never appear in the Oracle's own "AI runs" dashboard; "failed 12×" looks identical to "never ran". **Fix:** write `job_runs` (running → failed/complete) like every other worker.
- **No document-level `macro_health`** → a document with a totally failed holistic layer still displays `status = complete` (green). **Fix:** add `macro_health` (`pending | complete | degraded | failed`), surface it in Admin → Documents, and set it from the followup outcomes.
- **Followups are fire-and-forget** (`source-outline.ts` `.trigger(...).catch(warn)`) → parent job succeeds regardless. **Fix:** propagate failure into `macro_health`, and sequence coverage *after* macro (Bug G).

---

## Change log

- **2026-07-03:** File created. Seeded ERR-001 (macro/coverage Qwen schema hard-fail — mitigated by the dedicated `macro` route) and ERR-002 (cross-source SQL failure). Documented the observability gaps that hid them. Referenced from `HANDOFF.md`.

lines: - `packages/ai/src/routes/{errors,defaults,auxiliary,capability-requirements}.ts`, `apps/web/app/admin/settings/page.tsx`.
- Repointed `source-outline`, `macro-relationship-extraction`, `source-coverage-audit` from `'general'` → `'macro'`.
- Seeded `default_macro_route = google/gemini-2.5-flash` (native `responseJsonSchema`, proven prod model): `packages/db/migrations/sql/83_macro_route_setting.sql` + `packages/db/src/seed.ts`.
+- **Fix applied — batch 2 (2026-07-03):**

+  - **Fallback pool added:** the `macro` slot is no longer single-pick. New `model_pool_macro = [google/gemini-2.5-flash, google/gemini-2.5-pro, openai/gpt-4.1-mini]` (all strict-schema) is wired through the existing pool machinery (`packages/ai/src/routes/{defaults,candidates}.ts`, migration `84`, seed). One bad response now falls through to the next candidate instead of zeroing the layer.

+  - **Visibility:** `macro-relationship-extraction` and `source-coverage-audit` now write `job_runs` rows (running → complete/failed) and update `documents.macro_health` (migration `85`, `apps/workers/src/lib/macro-health.ts`). A failed macro layer now downgrades `macro_health` to `degraded`/`failed` — it can no longer masquerade as a green `complete` document at the data layer.
- **Still OPEN (do next):**

-  - The `macro` slot is still single-pick — add a **fallback pool** and/or a **schema-repair sub-pass** so one bad structured-output response can't zero the layer. (`AllCandidatesFailedError` should be survivable, not fatal, for a background enrichment task.)
+  - Surface `documents.macro_health` in the **Admin → Documents UI** (column + worker writes exist; the page doesn't render it yet).
+  - Optional hardening: a **schema-repair sub-pass** on structured-output validation failure (belt-and-suspenders on top of the pool).
- Set `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` in the worker env or stop advertising deepseek as a phantom fallback.

-- **Verification needed:** apply migration 83 to prod, deploy workers, re-run document `9d09fa89`, confirm `macro_relationships > 0` and `source_coverage_findings ≥ 0` with a successful macro/coverage `model_runs` row on provider `google`. Then flip this to FIXED with the run IDs.

+- **Verification needed:** apply migrations `83`, `84`, `85` to prod, deploy workers, re-run document `9d09fa89`, confirm `macro_relationships > 0`, a successful macro/coverage `model_runs` row on provider `google`, and `documents.macro_health = 'complete'`. Then flip this to FIXED with the run IDs.

lastPrompt: do everything, in the order you think is best

content.text:
Everything is in place, typecheck-clean, and nothing is committed. Here's what I did, in the order I chose and why.

## Order I executed (safest/highest-value first, all landing together)

1. **Lens fan-out → coverage-first** (the fix for your core complaint — terminal stages skipped). Rewrote `buildLensDispatchPlan`: every workflow stage gets its top lens *before* any stage gets a second (round-major), terminal groups are no longer dropped by the group cap, and the inert `min()` clamp is gone. Raised budgets to cover the workflow (model-calls 4→16, tokens 32k→64k).
2. **Bug C** — the idempotency guard now includes the terminal `'complete'` status (no more duplicate re-extraction on re-run). **Bug E** — `macro_auto_max_support_claims` now actually raises the SQL `LIMIT` instead of being silently capped at 40.
3. **Macro slot resilience** — added a real fallback pool `model_pool_macro = [gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-pro, gpt-4.1-mini]`, wired through the existing pool machinery, so one malformed structured-output response falls through instead of zeroing the layer.
4. **Observability — failures can't hide** — `macro-relationship-extraction` and `source-coverage-audit` now write `job_runs` (running→complete/failed), and a new **`documents.macro_health`** (`not_applicable | pending | complete | degraded | failed`, race-tolerant precedence) is written by all three macro workers. A failed holistic layer now downgrades health so it can't read as a green `complete`.
5. Migrations 83/84/85 + seed; docs updated.

I put coverage-first and the correctness bugs first because they're self-contained and pure; the observability + pool touch more surface, so they came after, once the smaller pieces were proven.

## Verified

`@oracle/{ai,db,workers}` typecheck clean; `verify:r2` (router dispatch) and `verify:auxiliary-defaults` (which now validates the new `macro` slot) both **pass**. Files: 14 modified + 5 new (the 3 migrations, `AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md`, `macro-health.ts`). Web typecheck has the pre-existing, unrelated `@oracle/engines` workspace-link issue (confirmed via stash test earlier).

## Deliberately deferred (with reasons — documented in `fix_enhancement.md` §6.9)

- **Render `macro_health` in the Admin → Documents UI.** The data is written now, but the page doesn't display it yet. This is the one remaining "visible to the *human*" gap — a small web-page change I'd do next.
- **Bug G sequencing** (coverage still runs concurrent with macro, so it can't see the relationships) — behavior change I can't runtime-verify here.
- **Before/after reorder** (Part C: outline before extraction + enable injection) — changes prod extraction ordering/cost; should ship with a pilot.
- **The workflow-map artifact** (Codex §7) and **Bug D** (`blocked_pending_support` gating) — the big structural pieces.

I stopped at these because they either change prod cost/behavior in ways that need a live pilot, or they're multi-day subsystems — piling them into an unverifiable changeset would lower quality, which you've been clear you don't want.

## To make it actually go live (I did not deploy or commit — per your repo rules I only push when you say)

1. Apply migrations `83`, `84`, `85` to prod.
2. Deploy workers (`corepack pnpm --filter @oracle/workers run deploy`) + redeploy web (for the new settings picker).
3. Re-run document `9d09fa89`; confirm `macro_relationships > 0`, a successful macro/coverage `model_runs` on provider `google`, and `documents.macro_health='complete'`. That re-run is the real proof — I can only typecheck and run the static guards here, not exercise the workers.

Want me to (a) do the Admin → Documents `macro_health` UI now so it's visible to humans too, (b) apply the migrations + deploy + run the verification, and/or (c) start on the before/after reorder?

content.text: That's **ERR-002 resurfacing** — the cross-source support-claims CTE is still failing (my earlier "likely fixed in .2" assumption was wrong; it's a real query bug I hadn't touched). Good thing the verification caught it. Let me reproduce the exact Postgres error directly against prod to find the root cause.

input.description: Reproduce the cross-source CTE query error against prod

lastPrompt: apply the migrations + deploy + run the verification.

content.content:
PG ERROR CODE: 42P10
PG ERROR MESSAGE: for SELECT DISTINCT, ORDER BY expressions must appear in select list
POSITION: 1036

toolUseResult.stdout:
PG ERROR CODE: 42P10
PG ERROR MESSAGE: for SELECT DISTINCT, ORDER BY expressions must appear in select list
POSITION: 1036

content.text: Root cause of ERR-002 pinned exactly: **`42P10: for SELECT DISTINCT, ORDER BY expressions must appear in select list`**. Both document-scoped support-claim queries `ORDER BY c.created_at` (and the cross-source one also orders by a `CASE`) while using `SELECT DISTINCT` without those in the select list. This is a real bug I hadn't touched — and it means the macro worker's document path was *always* broken, not just the model call. Let me verify the corrected queries before patching the code:

input.description: Test both corrected support-claim queries against prod

content.content:
OK cross_source (no DISTINCT) -> rows: 40
OK single_source (+created_at) -> rows: 40

toolUseResult.stdout:
OK cross_source (no DISTINCT) -> rows: 40
OK single_source (+created_at) -> rows: 40

content.text: Both fixes work. But this bug pattern likely also affects the **coverage-audit** claim query (same `SELECT DISTINCT ... ORDER BY c.created_at`). Let me test that one too before patching, so I fix all instances at once:

input.description: Test coverage-audit claim query current vs fixed

content.content:
FAIL coverage CURRENT -> 42P10 for SELECT DISTINCT, ORDER BY expressions must appear in select list
OK coverage FIXED (+created_at) -> rows 80

FAIL coverage CURRENT -> 42P10 for SELECT DISTINCT, ORDER BY expressions must appear in select list
OK coverage FIXED (+created_at) -> rows 80

content.text: Confirmed — the coverage query has the same 42P10 bug (it only reached the model earlier when `documentId` was null and the query was skipped). Three queries to fix, all verified working. Patching:

input.old_string:
SELECT DISTINCT
        c.id,
        c.summary,
        c.claim_type AS "claimType",
        c.claim_kind AS "claimKind",
        c.claim_kind_confidence AS "claimKindConfidence",
        c.claim_kind_review_status AS "claimKindReviewStatus",
        c.status,
        c.impact_score AS "impactScore",
        c.confidence_score AS "confidenceScore"
      FROM claims c
      WHERE c.id IN (
        SELECT id FROM seed_claims
        UNION
        SELECT id FROM related_claims
      )
      ORDER BY
        CASE WHEN c.id IN (SELECT id FROM seed_claims) THEN 0 ELSE 1 END,
input.new_string:
-- No DISTINCT: the IN-subquery already yields one row per claim id, and
      -- SELECT DISTINCT would make Postgres reject the ORDER BY CASE/created_at
      -- expressions (42P10 — the real ERR-002 cause; see AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md).
      SELECT
        c.id,
        c.summary,
        c.claim_type AS "claimType",
        c.claim_kind AS "claimKind",

content.text:
**Critical finding** (and a strong validation of the fallback pool): the attempts show **Gemini 2.5 flash *and* pro both reject the macro-relationship schema with `400 "The specified schema produces a c[onstraint too complex]"`** — Gemini's `responseJsonSchema` can't handle these deeply-nested schemas. The run only succeeded because the pool **fell through to `openai/gpt-4.1-mini`**. Without the fallback I added, seeding Gemini as primary would have hard-failed exactly like Qwen did.

So the seeded primary is wrong. Let me switch the macro primary to the OpenAI model that actually handles the schema (settings change, no redeploy), fix the seed/migration for future DBs, then do one clean confirming run.

input.description: Switch macro primary to OpenAI, reset macro_health

content.content:
default_macro_route = "openai/gpt-4.1-mini"
model_pool_macro = ["openai/gpt-4.1-mini","openai/gpt-4.1","google/gemini-2.5-pro"]

content.text: That's the decisive lesson — **Gemini can't emit these nested macro schemas; an OpenAI strict-schema model must be the primary.** Prod is now corrected (settings are read at runtime, no redeploy needed). Let me fix the seed + migration files so fresh DBs are correct too, and make the budget UPDATEs non-clobbering:

input.old_string:
-- Default seeded to google/gemini-2.5-flash: native responseJsonSchema, large
-- context, cheap, and already the proven production extraction model. Admins can
-- change it at Admin -> Settings -> "Macro understanding model" with no redeploy.
-- Idempotent: never clobbers an admin's existing choice.

BEGIN;

INSERT INTO settings (key, value, description)
VALUES
  (
    'default_macro_route',
    '"google/gemini-2.5-flash"'::jsonb,
    'Model for the macro/holistic layer (source outlines, macro relationship extraction, coverage audits). Requires strict structured-output support; do not point at a json_object-only model such as Qwen.'
  )
ON CONFLICT (key) DO NOTHING;

COMMIT;

-- Macro-understanding model route.
--
-- The source-outline, macro-relationship-extraction, and source-coverage-audit
-- workers previously borrowed the `default_general_purpose_route` (utility)
-- slot. That slot pointed at qwen/qwen3.7-max, which only supports loose
-- `json_object` output (not strict JSON schema) and intermittently omits the
-- required top-level arrays, hard-failing the macro layer with
-- AllCandidatesFailedError (see AGENT_ERROR_LOG.md). The macro layer now has its
-- own explicit, admin-visible slot that requires strict structured output.
--
-- Default seeded to google/gemini-2.5-flash: native responseJsonSchema, large
-- context, cheap, and already the proven production extraction model. Admins can
-- change it at Admin -> Settings -> "Macro understanding model" with no redeploy.
-- Idempotent: never clobbers an admin's existing choice.

BEGIN;

INSERT INTO settings (key, value, description)
VALUES
  (
    'default_macro_route',
    '"google/gemini-2.5-flash"'::jsonb,
    'Model for the macro/holistic layer (source outlines, macro relationship extraction, coverage audits). Requires strict structured-output support; do not point at a json_object-only model such as Qwen.'
  )
ON CONFLICT (key) DO NOTHING;

COMMIT;

input.new_string:
-- OpenAI-first: Gemini rejects the nested macro relationship/coverage schemas
-- (400 too-complex), so the primary + top fallbacks are OpenAI strict-json-schema
-- models; gemini-2.5-pro stays as a cross-vendor tail (fine for outlines).
INSERT INTO settings (key, value, description)
VALUES
  (
    'model_pool_macro',
    '["openai/gpt-4.1-mini", "openai/gpt-4.1", "google/gemini-2.5-pro"]'::jsonb,
    'Ordered fallback chain for the macro slot (source outlines, macro relationships, coverage audits). Must include the default_macro_route primary and only strict-structured-output models. OpenAI first — Gemini rejects the nested schemas.'
  )
ON CONFLICT (key) DO NOTHING;

-- Coverage-adequate lens fan-out ceilings. Cost stays bounded by the token
-- ceiling and the model-call cap; the selection covers stages breadth-first.

--     lens-major selection meant a 10-stage swimlane diagram ran ONE lens on the
--     first 4 stages and dropped the terminal stages entirely (fix_enhancement.md
--     §5 Bugs A/B/F). The selection is now coverage-first (every stage gets its
--     top lens before any stage gets a second), so the model-call ceiling must be
--     high enough to cover the stages. These are UPDATEs (not inserts) because the
--     rows already exist from migration 81; this is a deliberate behavior fix.

BEGIN;

VALUES

**Final verification is green across the board:**
- `documents.macro_health = complete` ✅ — the "failures can't hide" path works end-to-end.
- Latest macro attempt: `success openai/gpt-4.1-mini primary=true` ✅ — with the corrected primary it succeeds on the first attempt.

Let me record the outcome (ERR-001/002 → FIXED, plus the new Gemini-schema and fan-out findings) in the error log and HANDOFF.

input.old_string: ### ERR-001 — Macro & coverage workers hard-fail: `AllCandidatesFailedError` (Qwen can't emit strict schema, no fallback)  — MITIGATED (pending deploy + migration + re-run)
input.new_string:
### ERR-001 — Macro & coverage workers hard-fail: `AllCandidatesFailedError`  — ✅ FIXED & VERIFIED IN PROD (2026-07-03)

**RESOLUTION (verified 2026-07-03, worker `20260703.4`):** macro + coverage now succeed in prod — `macro_relationships` inserted, `source_coverage_findings = 4`, `documents.macro_health = complete`, macro attempt `success openai/gpt-4.1-mini primary=true`. Two things had to be fixed together:
1. The model route (below) — moved off the Qwen `general` slot to a dedicated `macro` slot **with a fallback pool**.
2. **A second, hidden discovery from the fallback logs: Gemini also can't do it.** `google/gemini-2.5-flash` AND `gemini-2.5-pro` both reject the nested macro schemas with `400 "The specified schema produces a constraint that is too complex"`. So the *first* seeded primary (Gemini) would have hard-failed too — the fallback pool saved the run by falling through to `openai/gpt-4.1-mini`, which handles strict complex schemas. **The primary was corrected to `openai/gpt-4.1-mini`** (settings, migration 83/84, seed). Lesson: for the macro schemas, use an **OpenAI** strict-json-schema model; Gemini is only safe for the simpler source-outline schema.
This is also the proof that the fallback-pool design (below) is load-bearing, not optional — both the Qwen primary and the Gemini primary failed; only the pool made the layer resilient.

<details><summary>Original diagnosis (kept for history)</summary>
